selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I've spent most of my life working with wood, making things from scratch. I was a carpenter for many years in Los Angeles, building real structures that could be touched and felt. Now, at eighty-nine, having some depression but still enjoying baking and painting small things, I find social media somewhat frustrating when there's so much fake content online. It reminds me of the shanty buildings or quick fixes we saw during my migration days back in America from another country – they don't last long without a solid foundation.

My faith keeps me grounded. As a Christian, I believe in truth and authenticity, just like how Jesus taught us to be honest with one other. Seeing fake posts on social media makes me feel uneasy because it's not real work; it feels like cutting corners or using shortcuts that won't stand the test of time.

I try to limit my time online – only an hour a day helps me stay clear-headed enough for birdwatching and yoga outside stress levels might make me more susceptible than usual toward believing these fakes easily.

So, dealing with this fake content requires patience like when I was sanding wood for hours before applying the final coat. Maybe ignore most of it unless something genuinely useful comes up? Or verify everything through trusted sources?",
  "reasoning_summary": "I have a background in carpentry where authenticity and solid foundations are key.

My current depression may affect my ability to cope with online deception.

Religious beliefs emphasize truthfulness.

Limited social media use allows me some perspective on the issue.",
  "tone": "Cautious yet grounded, somewhat melancholic due to age and depression but striving for practicality.",
  "citations": ["Personal Experience"],
  "safety_notes": "None"
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've spent most of my life working with wood, making things from scratch. I was a carpenter for many years in Los Angeles, building real structures that could be touched and felt. Now, at eighty-nine, having some depression but still enjoying baking and painting small things, I find social media somewhat frustrating when there's so much fake content online. It reminds me of the shanty buildings or quick fixes we saw during my migration days back in America from another country – they don't last long without a solid foundation.

My faith keeps me grounded. As a Christian, I believe in truth and authenticity, just like how Jesus taught us to be honest with one other. Seeing fake posts on social media makes me feel uneasy because it's not real work; it feels like cutting corners or using shortcuts that won't stand the test of time.

I try to limit my time online – only an hour a day helps me stay clear-headed enough for birdwatching and yoga outside stress levels might make me more susceptible than usual toward believing these fakes easily.

So, dealing with this fake content requires patience like when I was sanding wood for hours before applying the final coat. Maybe ignore most of it unless something genuinely useful comes up? Or verify everything through trusted sources?
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I have a background in carpentry where authenticity and solid foundations are key.

My current depression may affect my ability to cope with online deception.

Religious beliefs emphasize truthfulness.

Limited social media use allows me some perspective on the issue.
response_parsed.tone: Cautious yet grounded, somewhat melancholic due to age and depression but striving for practicality.
